name: Begonia amphioxus
origin: Sabah, northern Borneo (humid forest, moist limestone outcrops)
aboutText: Begonia amphioxus, described by Sands, is one of the most visually dramatic begonias in cultivation — a rare upright species from the humid tropical forests of Sabah in northern Borneo, typically found on moist limestone outcrops, humus-rich soil and decaying leaf litter in warm, constantly humid, shaded understory conditions. The slender, segmented red canes carry narrow, lanceolate leaves of olive to bronzy green bearing vivid red venation, red margins and numerous silvery-pink spots, with undersides entirely red. The combination of the cane habit, the narrow pointed leaf form and the exuberant spotting and venation makes amphioxus one of the most sought-after Begonia species worldwide — a plant that commands immediate attention from any serious collector and considerable prices to match.

morphology.venation: Vivid red venation against bronzy-olive blade
morphology.texture: Thin-coriaceous with silvery-pink spots and red undersides
morphology.variegation: Silvery-pink spots, red venation, red margins (natural species markings)
morphology.growthHabit: Upright segmented red canes with airy branching habit

propagation.notes: Most rhizomatous begonias root readily from leaf sections or petiole cuttings placed on moist substrate. Keep warm and humid until plantlets emerge at leaf margins.

careGuide.substrate: Light, well-draining mix: 50% perlite, 30% potting compost, 20% orchid bark. Begonias are susceptible to root rot — excellent drainage is essential.
careGuide.watering: Allow the top half of the substrate to dry between waterings. Water at the base — wet leaves invite fungal problems. Reduce in winter.
careGuide.humidity: 50–70%. More tolerant of average indoor humidity than tropical aroids, but consistent moisture prevents crispy leaf margins.
careGuide.fertilising: Balanced liquid fertiliser at quarter strength every 2–3 weeks during the growing season. Do not feed in winter.
careGuide.repotting: Every 12–18 months in spring. Begonias like shallow, wide pots that suit rhizomatous growth. Don't bury the rhizome.

commonProblems.cause: Poor air circulation combined with high humidity
commonProblems.fix: Improve airflow; avoid wetting leaves; treat with a dilute bicarbonate of soda spray

commonProblems.cause: Overwatering, dead leaves left on plant, poor air circulation
commonProblems.fix: Remove dead foliage promptly; increase airflow; reduce watering
Move to a brighter position — begonias need good indirect light to maintain compact growth

items: Check for powdery mildew — a white dusty coating on leaf surfaces, especially in dry conditions; it spreads rapidly between plants
Inspect for mealybugs at leaf axils and the underside of stems where they hide in white cottony deposits
Rhizome begonias should show firm, healthy rhizome tissue — avoid plants with soft, discoloured rhizomes
Most begonias ship well as leaf cuttings — if buying a cutting, ensure it has a healthy petiole and no signs of rot at the cut end
